items.title: Most common cause: alcoholism
items.contentHtml:
Most common cause: alcoholism

  • Chronic alcoholism (>80 g/day in men, >60 g/day in women for >6-12 years) is the most common cause of chronic pancreatitis in Western countries, responsible for 60-70% of cases.
  • The direct toxicity of ethanol and its metabolites (acetaldehyde) induces stress oxidative stress, periacinar fibrosis and intrapancreatic fat necrosis, with irreversible loss of parenchyma.
  • The risk is dose-dependent; consumption >150 g/day increases the probability 5-10 times compared to non-drinkers.
  • In ENARM/EUNACOM, the classic question: "Male patient 45 years old, chronic drinker, epigastric pain with steatorrhea", the answer is alcoholic chronic pancreatitis.

items.foco: Epigastric pain radiating to the back
items.title: Epigastric pain radiating to the back
items.contentHtml:
Epigastric pain radiating to the back

  • Epigastric pain radiating to the back is the cardinal symptom of chronic pancreatitis; typically postprandial, dull, intense, relieved by leaning forward (Mallory-Weiss sign does not apply; it is antalgic posture).
  • Irradiation is due to the involvement of the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves due to retroperitoneal inflammation of the pancreas.
  • In advanced stages, the pain can be constant and difficult to control; Up to 30% of patients present pain independent of intake.
  • Key differential diagnosis: perforated peptic ulcer (more acute pain, contracture), acute cholecystitis (Murphy sign), recurrent acute pancreatitis (elevated amylase/lipase >3 times the upper limit).

items.title: Pancreatic calcifications in imaging
items.contentHtml:
Pancreatic calcifications on image

  • Pancreatic calcifications are the pathognomonic sign of advanced chronic pancreatitis, present in 50-90% of cases, visible on CT without contrast (sensitivity ~80%) or simple abdominal x-ray (less sensitive).
  • They represent calcium carbonate deposits within the pancreas. pancreatic ducts and parenchyma secondary to thick secretion and protein plugs.
  • In ERCP they are seen as filling defects; on ultrasound they appear as posterior acoustic shadows.
  • Trap examination: calcifications can be single or multiple, linear along the Wirsung duct; Its absence does not rule out early chronic pancreatitis (Cambridge stage I).

items.title: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency
items.contentHtml:
Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency

  • Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency appears when >90% of acinar function is lost; It manifests itself as steatorrhea (fatty, voluminous, malodorous stools) and deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
  • The fecal elastase test (monoclonal ELISA) is the most used: values ​​<200 µg/g indicate severe insufficiency; <100 µg/g is severe. Sensitivity ~80% in advanced disease.
  • Other tests: secretin-cholecystokinin test (gold standard, invasive) measures bicarbonate and enzymes in duodenal juice; in chronic pancreatitis there is a decrease in bicarbonate secretion <80 mEq/L.
  • 1st therapeutic line: pancreatic enzymes (lipase 25,000-50,000 IU with each meal) plus proton pump inhibitor to optimize enzymatic activity.

items.title: Secondary diabetes mellitus
items.contentHtml:
Secondary diabetes mellitus

  • Secondary diabetes mellitus to chronic pancreatitis (pancreopriva diabetes or type 3c) occurs in 30-50% of patients, due to destruction of islets of Langerhans and fibrosis.
  • It is characterized by insulin deficiency and deficiency. glucagon, which generates a low insulin requirement (dose <0.5 U/kg/day) and a tendency to frequent hypoglycemia.
  • The differential diagnosis with type 2 DM: presence of steatorrhea, pancreatic calcifications and absence of autoantibodies (anti-GAD, ICA).
  • Treatment: insulin (prefer long-acting analogues); Metformin is not first line due to the risk of lactic acidosis if there is severe exocrine insufficiency.

items.title: Wirsung duct dilation
items.contentHtml:
Dilation of the Wirsung duct

  • The dilation of the Wirsung duct (>3-4 mm in head, >2 mm in tail) is a typical finding in chronic pancreatitis, secondary to obstruction by protein stones or stenosis.
  • In ERCP or cholangio-MRI it is classified according to the Cambridge scale: grade I (mild) with dilation <3 mm; grade II (moderate) with irregularities; grade III (severe) with dilation >5 mm and calcifications.
  • The presence of irregular duct with saccular dilation and loss of arborization of the secondary ducts is pathognomonic.
  • Trap: in chronic pancreatitis the dilation can be diffuse or segmental; The absence of dilation does not exclude early disease.

items.title: Low fecal elastase test
items.contentHtml:
Low fecal elastase test

  • The low fecal elastase test (<200 µg/g) is the non-invasive test of choice for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency in chronic pancreatitis; uses monoclonal antibodies against human elastase-1.
  • Advantages: it is not affected by the use of exogenous pancreatic enzymes (unlike fecal chymotrypsin), nor by food intake; shows high specificity (>90%) in advanced disease.
  • Limitations: false positives in watery diarrhea (dilution) and in mild exocrine insufficiency (sensitivity ~60%).
  • Diagnostic threshold: <200 µg/g = moderate insufficiency; <100 µg/g = severe deficiency; <15 µg/g suggests almost total fibrosis.

items.title: Steatorrhea due to poor digestion
items.contentHtml:
Steatorrhea due to maldigestion

  • steatorrhea due to maldigestion in chronic pancreatitis occurs when lipase secretion is <10% of normal; It is defined as excretion of fecal fat >7 g/day in 72 hours (Van de Kamer test).
  • Clinically: pale, greasy, smelly, floating stools that are difficult to eliminate (they stick to the toilet).
  • Associated with a deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins: vitamin D (osteomalacia), vitamin K (INR prolongation), vitamin A (night blindness), vitamin E (neuropathy).
  • Differential diagnosis: celiac disease (anti-transglutaminase antibodies), bacterial overgrowth (breath hydrogen test), ileal Crohn's disease (colonoscopy).

items.title: Associated pancreatic pseudocysts
items.contentHtml:
Associated pancreatic pseudocysts

  • The pancreatic pseudocysts are encapsulated liquid collections without epithelium, which appear in 20-40% of chronic pancreatitis, generally secondary to ductal rupture or necrosis.
  • They are located in the retroperitoneum (body/tail) and can communicate with the duct. Wirsung; the diagnosis is confirmed with CT or ERCP (shows ductal communication).
  • Drainage criteria: size >5-6 cm, persistence >6 weeks, gastric/duodenal compression, infection, hemorrhage or rupture.
  • 1st line of treatment if symptomatic: endoscopic drainage (cystogastrostomy or cystoduodenostomy) with a plastic stent; Surgery (cystojejunostomy) is reserved for failures.

items.title: Smoking as a risk factor
items.contentHtml:
Smoking as a risk factor

  • Smoking is an independent and dose-dependent risk factor for chronic pancreatitis; smoking >20 cigarettes/day increases the risk 2-3 times compared to non-smokers.
  • Tobacco smoke contains nitrosamines and free radicals that promote pancreatic fibrosis and calcifications, even in the absence of alcoholism.
  • In cohort studies, smoking accelerates the progression of chronic pancreatitis and worsens exocrine insufficiency and endocrine.
  • Examination trap: in idiopathic chronic pancreatitis, smoking may be the only identifiable factor; Always ask about smoking habits.

items.title: Diagnosis with CT or ERCP
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis with CT or ERCP

  • The CT of the abdomen with contrast is the initial imaging technique of choice for chronic pancreatitis; detects pancreatic calcifications, atrophy, dilation of the Wirsung duct and pseudocysts with 75-90% sensitivity. ERCP (endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography) is the gold standard for evaluating ductal morphology: it shows irregularities, dilation, stenosis and stones; classified according to Cambridge (grades I-IV).
  • MRI cholangio-cholangio (MRCP) is a non-invasive alternative with ~85% sensitivity for ductal changes, but lower for small calcifications.
  • Indication for ERCP: if treatable ductal obstruction is suspected (stenosis, stones) or for cytological sampling if there is doubt of neoplasia.

items.title: Treatment: analgesia and enzymes
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: analgesia and enzymes

  • The treatment of pain in chronic pancreatitis follows the WHO analgesic ladder: initially paracetamol or NSAIDs; if it persists, minor opioids (tramadol 50-100 mg every 6 hours) and then major opioids (morphine, oxycodone).
  • Pancreatic enzymes (pancrelipase) not only treat steatorrhea but can reduce pain by inhibiting negative feedback (decrease in cholecystokinin). Dose: 25,000-50,000 IU of lipase with each meal, plus 10,000-25,000 IU with snacks.
  • In refractory pain, consider celiac plexus block (ultrasound or CT guidance) or surgery (Puesow: lateral pancreaticojejunostomy for dilated duct >7 mm).
  • 1st line for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency: pancreatic enzymes plus PPI (omeprazole 20-40 mg/day) to avoid acid degradation.
